ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a longan entry but does not have a specific node for dried/dehydrated longan without skin; the closest parent node is used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A00LC covers dried longan as a processed fruit; skin-removed specification is not separately coded but is captured in the food name.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT code identified for dried longan without skin; SNOMED CT coverage of tropical dried fruits is limited.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of dried longan without skin is approximately 20 g (5–8 pieces), commonly used as a snack or added to herbal soups and sweet dessert soups.

nutrient_source.title: Dried longan, without skin — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Core macros and available micronutrients (energy, protein, fat, carbohydrate, sodium, potassium, calcium, iron, cholesterol) sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for dried longan without skin (cr_id F04030021) at confidence 0.95. Sugar_g, saturated_fat_g, and dietary_fibre_g cross-referenced from USDA FoodData Central FDC ID 168151 (Longans, dried) as HPB does not separately report these fractions.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — dried fruit analogous estimate; longan dried approximated from dried lychee and dried tropical fruit cluster (GI ~55)
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS et al., Diabetes Care 2021
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for dried longan without skin exists in the University of Sydney GI Database as of April 2026. GI of 55 is estimated by analogy with dried lychee and other dried tropical fruits with comparable sugar content (~66 g/100 g) and low fibre (~1.1 g/100 g). A GI of 55 falls at the upper boundary of the low GI band (≤55) per the standard University of Sydney classification (low ≤55, medium 56–69, high ≥70). Glycemic load per 20 g serving = (55 × 74 × 0.20) / 100 = 8.1 g. Input record confidence for GI was 0.346, reflecting this estimated status.

hypertension.key_factor: Very high sugar content (66 g/100 g) with low dietary fibre (1.1 g/100 g) drives insulin-mediated sodium retention, partially offsetting the benefit of high potassium (658 mg/100 g); sodium is low at 48 mg/100 g.
hypertension.guidance: Limit to 3–5 pieces (≈15–20 g) per serving to keep sugar load clinically manageable; the high potassium content is beneficial for blood pressure, but large portions negate this advantage through sugar-driven insulin-mediated sodium retention.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Estimated sugar of 66 g/100 g far exceeds the 20 g/100 g glycaemic ceiling; low dietary fibre (1.1 g/100 g) provides minimal buffering, resulting in rapid and significant postprandial blood glucose elevation.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Avoid as a regular snack; if used in herbal soups, restrict to 3–5 pieces per pot and do not consume the cooking liquid in large quantities, as the concentrated natural sugars cause rapid blood glucose spikes unsuitable for glycaemic management.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Cholesterol-free and low in fat (0.4 g/100 g) with high potassium (658 mg/100 g) and meaningful iron (5.4 mg/100 g), but the very high sugar content (66 g/100 g) raises triglyceride risk at large portion sizes.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Small portions (≈15–20 g) are acceptable within a heart-healthy diet given the absence of cholesterol and low fat content, but regular large-portion consumption should be avoided due to the high sugar load that may elevate triglycerides and contribute to metabolic cardiovascular risk.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Estimated sugar of 66 g/100 g exceeds the 15 g/100 g RA threshold, promoting pro-inflammatory cytokine activity; fibre is very low at 1.1 g/100 g and no meaningful anti-inflammatory factor is present.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Use only 3–5 pieces as a flavouring agent in herbal soups rather than consuming in quantity; pair with anti-inflammatory ingredients such as ginger or wolfberries to partially offset the pro-inflammatory sugar load.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Dried longan without skin (桂圆干 / 龙眼干) is a dehydrated tropical fruit produced from the longan (Dimocarpus longan), a member of the soapberry family widely cultivated in southern China, Thailand, Vietnam, and Southeast Asia. The fresh fruit is peeled, pitted, and then dried — either sun-dried or oven-dried — to produce a translucent, golden-brown, chewy flesh with an intensely sweet, caramel-like flavour. The drying process concentrates the natural sugars dramatically, yielding approximately 66 g of sugar per 100 g and a total carbohydrate content of 74 g/100 g, compared to roughly 15 g/100 g in fresh longan. Per 100 g, dried longan without skin provides 286 kcal, 4.9 g protein, 0.4 g fat, 74 g carbohydrate, 1.1 g dietary fibre, 48 mg sodium, 658 mg potassium, 45 mg calcium, and 5.4 mg iron. The high potassium and iron content make it nutritionally notable among dried fruits. In Chinese culinary and traditional medicine traditions, dried longan (known as guì yuán or 桂圆) is used in herbal soups, sweet dessert soups (糖水), tonic teas, and red date preparations, and is believed to nourish the blood and calm the mind. It is a common ingredient in Singaporean, Malaysian, and Chinese household cooking. Clinically, the very high sugar concentration (66 g/100 g) and low fibre (1.1 g/100 g) make it unsuitable for regular consumption by individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes, hypertension with insulin resistance, or rheumatoid arthritis. Portion control is essential: a typical serving of 5–8 pieces (≈20 g) delivers approximately 14.8 g of sugar and 8.1 g of glycaemic load. The glycaemic index is estimated at 55 (low, at the upper boundary of the low GI band) by analogy with similar concentrated-sugar dried tropical fruits. It is naturally cholesterol-free, vegan, halal, gluten-free, and dairy-free.
common_user_queries: Is dried longan high in sugar?

confidence_reason: Core macronutrient and micronutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id F04030021) at confidence 0.95. Sugar_g, saturated_fat_g, and dietary_fibre_g cross-referenced from USDA FoodData Central (FDC ID 168151). GI is an analogous estimate (confidence 0.346) from the University of Sydney GI Database cluster for dried tropical fruits; this is clearly documented. All other fields are derived from approved sources or defensible analogy.
notes: 1. Sugar_g (66.0 g/100 g) is estimated from the carbohydrate profile of dried longan as reported in the HPB database and cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central entry for dried longan (FDC ID 168151); the HPB source does not separately report sugar, so 66 g/100 g is used as a well-supported estimate consistent with USDA data. 2. Saturated_fat_g (0.1 g/100 g) is estimated from USDA FoodData Central dried longan data (FDC ID 168151); HPB does not separately report saturated fat. 3. Dietary_fibre_g (1.1 g/100 g) is estimated from USDA FoodData Central dried longan data (FDC ID 168151); HPB does not separately report fibre. 4. Cholesterol_mg is 0.0 — confirmed zero per HPB source; dried longan is a plant-derived food and contains no cholesterol. 5. The following fields are set to -1 (data not available): monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g. Reason: detailed fatty acid fractions (monounsaturated, polyunsaturated, trans), full vitamin panel, mineral panel (phosphorus, magnesium, zinc, selenium), and moisture data are not reported in the HPB Foodo database entry for this food (cr_id F04030021). While partial data exists in USDA FoodData Central (FDC ID 168151), the HPB record is the primary approved source for this Singapore-context food and these fields are not present in that record. These fields are flagged for future enrichment from USDA FoodData Central. 6. GI of 55 is an analogous estimate classified as low GI (≤55 per University of Sydney standard classification); no direct GI measurement for dried longan without skin exists in the University of Sydney GI Database as of April 2026. Glycaemic load per 20 g serving = 8.1 g. 7. This food is classified as a processed fruit (dehydrated); it is not ultra-processed as no additives are used in traditional preparation.

Updated gi_source.source_match_notes and llm_training.natural_language_description accordingly.
